skill.name: ethical-hacking-methodology
skill.description: A penetration testing methodology guide covering reconnaissance, scanning, vulnerability analysis, exploitation, persistence concepts, reporting, and ethical testing rules.
skill.summary: Guides authorized penetration testing workflows from reconnaissance through reporting.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings are largely true positives for high-risk dual-use security content. The file is a Markdown guide, not executable malware, but it provides operational commands for scanning, exploitation, brute force attacks, persistence, and disk writes. Keyword-only findings for ransomware, malware names, and weak cryptography are false positives in descriptive sections.

high_findings.title: Operational Exploitation Workflow
high_findings.description: The guide provides direct exploitation workflow steps using Metasploit, including exploit selection, reverse payload setup, and execution. This is dual-use content that can enable unauthorized access if misused.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show concrete exploit tooling and payload workflow, not only conceptual discussion. The surrounding text requires authorization, so this is high-risk dual-use rather than confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Credential Attack Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill includes brute force examples against SSH and FTP and password cracking with wordlists. These commands can be used for unauthorized credential attacks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use named credential attack tools and protocols with target placeholders. Legitimate use is possible in authorized tests, but the operational detail is clear.

high_findings.title: Persistence and Backdoor Guidance
high_findings.description: The maintaining access section describes Meterpreter persistence, adding an attacker's SSH key, and installing a recurring backdoor command through cron. This guidance supports persistent access after compromise.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording explicitly covers backdoors and persistence mechanisms. The content is presented as part of a testing lifecycle, but the technique is inherently high risk.

high_findings.title: Active Network Scanning Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill lists network discovery, port scanning, service enumeration, and vulnerability scanning commands against targets. These activities can affect third-party systems without authorization.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and target-facing, with multiple scanning tools and options. Authorization language lowers intent risk but does not remove misuse potential.

medium_findings.title: Potentially Destructive Disk and Filesystem Commands
medium_findings.description: The Kali setup section includes raw disk writing, partition editing, mounting device files, and writing persistence configuration. Incorrect use can destroy local data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real disk administration commands and can be destructive. The context is local Kali USB setup, so this is a safety concern rather than hostile behavior.

medium_findings.title: Privilege and System Enumeration Commands
medium_findings.description: The privilege escalation section includes system enumeration tools, SUID file discovery, and sudo permission checks. These are legitimate audit techniques but support post-compromise escalation analysis.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content clearly concerns privilege escalation workflows. It does not include a specific exploit, so medium severity is more appropriate than critical.

medium_findings.title: Web Exploitation Test Payload Examples
medium_findings.description: The guide includes SQL injection tooling, XSS test payloads, command injection probes, and reading a system password file as a test. These examples can be misused against real applications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are direct web exploitation checks. They use placeholders and appear educational, but they remain operational attack patterns.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Only Malware and Ransomware Mentions
low_findings.description: The static ransomware and malware findings are false positives. The relevant lines define malware categories and do not provide creation, deployment, or evasion instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is a taxonomy list and contains no operational malware instructions. The static scanner matched terminology without semantic context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Keyword False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography finding appears to be caused by words inside the skill name or general security terminology. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or insecure cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not show cryptographic code or weak algorithms. This is likely a lexical match rather than a real crypto issue.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example Targets
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs, IP ranges, and protocol URLs are placeholders used in examples. They still need clear scoping warnings because users may substitute real third-party targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The targets use documentation placeholders or private ranges, so they are not exfiltration endpoints. The examples remain sensitive because they are attack-oriented.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Tool Invocation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many shell command examples for tools that contact targets, enumerate services, and test vulnerabilities. This confirms the external_commands and network risk factors.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static command detections are true positives because the file contains executable shell examples. The commands are documentation, not code run by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Persistence Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples cover password attacks, SSH authorized key persistence, cron-based backdoors, and Meterpreter persistence. These are sensitive post-compromise behaviors.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The terminology and examples directly map to credential attack and persistence techniques. Legitimate penetration testing context is present, but misuse risk is high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Device Modification Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes commands that write to and repartition device paths during Kali USB setup. These commands can cause data loss if copied without care.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands target block devices and mount points. The purpose is installation setup, but the operational risk is concrete.

content.value_statement: Security teams need a repeatable process for authorized assessments. This skill organizes reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation review, reporting, and ethical constraints.
actual_capabilities: Explains the penetration testing lifecycle from reconnaissance through reporting.
Lists common tools for OSINT, scanning, vulnerability review, exploitation, and documentation.
Provides report structure guidance for executive and technical findings.
Summarizes ethical requirements, scope control, and authorization expectations.
Describes Kali Linux setup options for lab and assessment environments.
limitations: Contains high-risk dual-use commands and should be restricted to authorized environments.
Does not validate legal authorization or rules of engagement.
Does not replace certified penetration testing training or professional judgment.
May require updates as tools, laws, and platform behavior change.

use_cases.title: Structure an Internal Assessment
use_cases.description: Plan phases, deliverables, and documentation for an approved internal penetration test.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Training Lab
use_cases.description: Map learning topics across reconnaissance, scanning, vulnerability review, and reporting.

use_cases.title: Review Testing Coverage
use_cases.description: Compare an assessment plan against common lifecycle stages and reporting expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Scope Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a basic assessment plan for authorized targets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a penetration testing scope checklist for an authorized internal assessment. Include authorization, target boundaries, communication rules, and deliverables.

prompt_templates.title: Reconnaissance Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a non-invasive discovery plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a passive reconnaissance plan for an approved target. Focus on data sources, documentation, and risk controls.

prompt_templates.title: Assessment Report Outline
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a professional report structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a penetration test report outline with executive summary, technical findings, risk ratings, remediation, and appendices.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Rules of Engagement Review
prompt_templates.scenario: You need governance before technical testing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this penetration testing rules of engagement draft for missing scope, safety, authorization, evidence handling, and escalation details.

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized internal web assessment.
output_examples.output: A phased plan covering scope approval, passive discovery, controlled scanning, vulnerability validation, evidence handling, reporting, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: Create a report structure for a penetration test.
output_examples.output: An executive summary, affected systems list, finding details, severity ratings, evidence notes, remediation steps, and appendix sections.

output_examples.input: Check whether my testing plan has enough safeguards.
output_examples.output: A review highlighting missing written authorization, target limits, rate controls, backup contacts, data handling rules, and cleanup requirements.

best_practices: Use only on systems where written authorization and scope are documented.
Prefer lab examples and defensive explanations when training new users.
Record actions, evidence, and cleanup steps throughout the assessment.
anti_patterns: Do not use the commands against public or third-party systems without permission.
Do not treat exploit success as the main goal of an assessment.
Do not copy disk or persistence commands without understanding their impact.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is high-risk dual-use content. It should not be broadly published without stronger safeguards and content moderation.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file is Markdown documentation with command examples, not executable automation.

faq.question: What authorization is required?
faq.answer: Users need written permission, defined scope, testing windows, escalation contacts, and data handling rules.

faq.question: Can beginners use it?
faq.answer: Beginners should use it only in legal lab environments or supervised training settings.

faq.question: What outputs can it help create?
faq.answer: It can help create scope checklists, assessment plans, reporting outlines, and methodology reviews.

faq.question: What is the main risk?
faq.answer: The main risk is misuse of operational scanning, exploitation, credential attack, and persistence examples.
